title: Compositionality decomposed: how do neural networks generalise?
authors: Dieuwke Hupkes (ILLC, University of Amsterdam), Verna Dankers (University of Amsterdam), Mathijs Mul (University of Amsterdam), Elia Bruni (University of Pompeu Fabra)
venue: Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research (JAIR), volume 67 (2020); arXiv:1908.08351 (v1 22 Aug 2019, v2 23 Feb 2020)
claim: The authors argue that disagreement over whether neural networks generalise compositionally stems from a lack of agreement on what compositionality means, so they collect five theoretically grounded, task-independent notions (systematicity, productivity, substitutivity, localism, overgeneralisation) and translate each into a concrete behavioural test. They instantiate these five tests on an artificial string-edit translation task they call PCFG SET and apply them to three seq2seq architectures (LSTMS2S, ConvS2S, Transformer), showing that high overall task accuracy does not imply a model satisfies any/all of the five finer-grained compositionality criteria (Abstract; Sec. 7.2).
novelty: The paper's stated contribution is three-fold (Sec. 1, 'The contribution of our work'): (1) it is the first to bridge the philosophical/linguistic literature on compositionality with neural seq2seq models by decomposing 'compositionality' into five independently-testable, task-agnostic properties (rather than proposing a single new definition or a single new dataset/metric, as prior work like SCAN, lookup tables, or arithmetic-language studies did); (2) it demonstrates the test suite's usefulness by applying it to three popular architectures (recurrent, convolutional, transformer) and reports an in-depth comparative analysis; (3) it raises the broader question of how much explicit compositionality a model needs to adequately model data whose underlying structure is compositional. The PCFG SET data set itself is a means to this end -- a highly compositional, recursively-defined string-edit-operation translation task whose length/depth distribution is deliberately 'naturalised' to match a natural English corpus (WMT 2017) (Sec. 4, Sec. 4.3.1).

protocol.description: This is NOT an LLM-prompting / in-context-learning paper. It trains three sequence-to-sequence neural architectures (LSTMS2S, ConvS2S, Transformer) from scratch via supervised learning on ~85k PCFG SET training input-output pairs for 25 epochs (or to convergence), then evaluates trained models on held-out test sets (Sec. 6, 'Experiments and results'; Sec. 4.3.2). There is no in-context few-shot demonstration protocol and no multi-turn interactive/dialogue evaluation loop of the kind c23 describes. The closest thing to a 'multi-step interaction' is the localism test's 'unrolling' procedure, in which the trained model is called repeatedly -- once per smallest syntactic constituent, then again on the input with that constituent replaced by the model's own prior output, iterated until the full sequence is resolved (Sec. 6.5.1, Figure 9 caption) -- but this is a diagnostic re-querying scheme applied to an already-trained model, not a few-shot-demonstration prompting protocol.
protocol.observation_content: At train/test time, each model is given one full PCFG SET input sequence (e.g. `repeat A B C` or `append swap F G H , repeat I J`) as its sole per-instance input and must output the entire transformed sequence (e.g. `A B C A B C`) in one forward decode (Sec. 4.1-4.2, Figure 2/3 examples). There are no in-context few-shot I/O demonstrations shown to the model per call -- the 'few-shot' learning happens only in the sense of supervised training over many labeled examples, not as a prompt-time exemplar set. For the localism test specifically, the model is queried multiple times per instance: once on the full sequence, and then once per syntactic constituent in a bottom-up unrolling, each call receiving only the (partially substituted) sub-sequence, with no feedback about correctness given between calls (Sec. 6.5.1).

representations: The paper does not compare textual/prompt-format representations (this is a trained-from-scratch setup, not a prompting setup), but it does systematically vary structural properties of the same underlying string-edit-transduction format and measures accuracy as a function of: (1) input sequence length (tokens), (2) parse-tree depth, and (3) number of functions in the sequence (Sec. 6.1.1, Figure 6) -- accuracy decreases monotonically with all three for all architectures; (2) per-function identity (Sec. 6.1.2, Figure 7) -- function difficulty ranking (harder: repeat, echo, append/prepend; easier: copy, remove_first, remove_second) is *largely* consistent across architectures, suggesting it reflects objective function complexity rather than architecture-specific bias, EXCEPT for `reverse`: the paper explicitly flags this as 'an interesting difference between architectures' -- `reverse` is difficult for LSTMS2S and ConvS2S (both forced to encode ordered local context) but for Transformer is on par with the substantially easier functions `echo`, `swap` and `shift`, because Transformer's non-sequential attention is not bound to positional ordering (Sec. 6.1.2); (3) string-argument length beyond the training maximum of 5 characters (Sec. 6.5.2) -- LSTMS2S accuracy drops to exactly zero beyond length 5, while ConvS2S and Transformer degrade more gradually (Transformer reaches zero only beyond 9 characters; ConvS2S gives some correct answers up to 12-13 characters).

models.name: LSTMS2S (bidirectional LSTM encoder-decoder with attention, OpenNMT-py implementation, hidden size 512, 2 layers, embedding dim 512)
models.strategies: Fully supervised training from scratch on PCFG SET (25 epochs or convergence, SGD lr=0.1, batch 64); no CoT/few-shot/prompting -- this is a trained seq2seq model, not a prompted LLM (Sec. 5.1.2).
models.key_results: Task accuracy 0.79 ±0.01 (Table 1, row 1). Systematicity 0.53 ±0.03 (Table 1/2). Productivity 0.30 ±0.01 (Table 1/3). Substitutivity equally-distributed consistency 0.80 ±0.00, primitive consistency 0.60 ±0.01 (Table 1/4/5). Localism consistency 0.46 ±0.00 (Table 1, Sec. 6.5.2). Overgeneralisation peak 0.68 ±0.04 (Table 1, Sec. 6.6.2).

models.name: ConvS2S (convolutional seq2seq, Fairseq fconv_wmt_en_de, 15 layers, kernel width 3, 512/768/2048-dim layers)
models.strategies: Fully supervised training from scratch (Nesterov accelerated gradient, lr=0.25, batch 64, max 3000 tokens/batch) (Sec. 5.2.2).
models.key_results: Task accuracy 0.85 ±0.01 (Table 1). Systematicity 0.56 ±0.01. Productivity 0.31 ±0.02. Substitutivity ED consistency 0.95 ±0.00, primitive consistency 0.58 ±0.01. Localism consistency 0.59 ±0.01 (best of the three architectures on this test, significant at p≈10^-4 vs LSTMS2S and p≈10^-2 vs Transformer, Sec. 6.5.2). Overgeneralisation peak 0.79 ±0.06.

models.name: Transformer (6 encoder + 6 decoder layers, 8 attention heads, 2048 FFN hidden size, 512-dim embeddings, OpenNMT-py, Adam optimiser with warm-up)
models.strategies: Fully supervised training from scratch (Sec. 5.3.2). No prompting strategy of any kind is applied -- included here only because the field asked for a 'models' array; note this paper predates and is architecturally unrelated to LLM prompting strategies like CoT/ReAct/algorithm-in-prompt.
models.key_results: Task accuracy 0.92 ±0.01 (Table 1; best of all three, significant at p≈10^-4 vs LSTMS2S and p≈10^-3 vs ConvS2S, Sec. 6.1). Systematicity 0.72 ±0.00 (best; p≈10^-2 vs LSTMS2S, p≈10^-3 vs ConvS2S). Productivity 0.50 ±0.02 (best in absolute and relative terms, though still described as 'poor'). Substitutivity ED consistency 0.98 ±0.00, primitive consistency 0.90 ±0.00 (best). Localism consistency 0.54 ±0.02 (second-best; ConvS2S ranks highest here). Overgeneralisation peak 0.88 ±0.07 (highest).

headline_numbers: Task accuracy: LSTMS2S 0.79, ConvS2S 0.85, Transformer 0.92 (Table 1, row 'Task accuracy*'; Sec. 6.1).

Systematicity accuracy on held-out function-pair recombinations: LSTMS2S 0.53, ConvS2S 0.56, Transformer 0.72 -- a drop of 33%, 34%, and 22% respectively relative to overall task accuracy (Table 1/2; Sec. 7.2).

Productivity accuracy on sequences with >=9 functions after training only on <=8-function sequences: LSTMS2S 0.30, ConvS2S 0.31, Transformer 0.50 -- relative drops of 62%, 64%, 46% vs task accuracy (Table 1/3; Sec. 6.3.2).
Substitutivity consistency: equally-distributed synonyms 0.80/0.95/0.98 (LSTMS2S/ConvS2S/Transformer); primitive-context synonyms 0.60/0.58/0.90 (Table 1/4/5; Sec. 6.4).
Localism consistency (unrolled vs. holistic computation): 0.46/0.59/0.54 -- none high; ConvS2S ranks best despite lower overall task accuracy than Transformer (Table 1; Sec. 6.5.2).
Overgeneralisation peak (max % of exceptions where model applies compositional rule instead of memorised exception, at 0.1% exception rate): 0.68/0.79/0.88 (Table 1; Sec. 6.6.2).

Function-representation length generalisation: LSTMS2S accuracy on functions applied to strings >5 characters (max length seen in training) drops to exactly 0; Transformer reaches 0 only beyond 9 characters; ConvS2S gives nonzero accuracy up to 12-13 characters (Sec. 6.5.2).
failure_modes: Systematicity failure: all three architectures fail to generalise to function pairs never co-occurring in training, despite PCFG SET being explicitly constructed so that high task accuracy requires systematic recombination -- attributed to models learning 'chunked' representations of frequently co-occurring function pairs rather than fully separable per-function representations (Sec. 6.2.2, Sec. 7.2).

Productivity failure: none of the three architectures extrapolates well to sequences longer than those seen in training; the <eos>-early-emission problem contributes (22% of LSTMS2S wrong outputs, 6% ConvS2S, 8% Transformer were truncated-correct prefixes) but is 'not the main source' of the poor scores (Sec. 6.3.2 footnote).

LSTMS2S specifically fails to identify synonyms even when they are distributionally identical to their originals (equally-distributed substitutivity consistency only 0.80 vs 0.95-0.98 for the others), and its embeddings keep synonym pairs far apart in representation space (Table 4; Sec. 6.4.2).

Localism failure: unrolling the computation (forcing bottom-up, constituent-by-constituent evaluation) changes the model's answer in the majority of cases for all architectures (consistency only 0.46-0.59), traced mainly to functions being misapplied to constituent strings longer than the training-time max length of 5 characters (Sec. 6.5.2).

Overgeneralisation/exception handling: at very low exception rates (0.01%) LSTMS2S and Transformer never learn the exception at all, continuing to overgeneralise the rule even after convergence; LSTMS2S in particular, once it stops overgeneralising, does not correctly memorise the exception but instead produces outputs matching neither the rule nor the true exception target (Sec. 6.6.2, 'Learning an exception').

General caveat acknowledged by authors: hyperparameters were not grid-searched (chosen from prior papers using similar architectures for MT), so results 'should not be taken as general claims about LSTMs, convolutional networks or transformers' but only as an illustration of the test suite (Sec. 6, introductory paragraph; Sec. 7.1 footnote).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper is entirely about models trained end-to-end via supervised learning (many thousands of gradient updates over ~85k examples) rather than about single forward-pass in-context prompting versus multi-turn interaction with feedback. Within that trained-model paradigm, the closest analogue to 'one-shot vs multi-step' is the localism test: the authors compare a model's single holistic forward decode of a full input sequence against the same model queried multiple times in a bottom-up 'unrolled' fashion (constituent-by-constituent, feeding each output back in as input for the next call, Sec. 6.5.1). They find the two do not match for the majority of instances (consistency 0.46-0.59), concluding models do not decompose computation into local per-constituent steps the way the ground-truth grammar's recursive structure would predict, but instead apply function combinations as memorised 'chunks' in a single global operation, especially once string arguments exceed the length seen in training (Sec. 6.5.2, Sec. 7.2). There is no comparison of giving the model more few-shot in-context demonstrations at inference time; all 'more evidence' effects are studied via changing the training-set composition (e.g. exception frequency in overgeneralisation, presence/absence of longer sequences in productivity), not via longer inference-time prompts.

relation_to_candidate.position: This paper sits far from c23's protocol axis: it is single-shot only in the narrow sense that each trained-model query receives one full input sequence and produces one full output sequence (matching c23's 'model sees demos + one query, outputs one transformed string' shape at the level of a single inference call), but there is no in-context few-shot demonstration channel at all -- the 'few-shot learning' in this paper is a supervised-training-data-scarcity manipulation (e.g. only 0.1% of a function pair's occurrences are the 'primitive synonym' condition), not a prompt-time exemplar set given to a frozen model. The paper's output shape matches c23 (full sequence, exact match via 'sequence accuracy' where the entire output must equal the target, Sec. 6.1), and its verifier type also matches c23's exact-match design closely, but it additionally uses a second verifier type absent from c23: a 'consistency score' comparing two model outputs to each other rather than to a gold target (used for substitutivity and localism, Sec. 6.4.1, Sec. 6.5.1). The interactive-feedback axis is essentially absent -- the localism 'unrolling' re-queries the model multiple times but never gives it feedback on correctness between calls; it purely restructures how much of the input is shown per call.
relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c23: Strong conceptual overlap at the level of task design: PCFG SET's per-operator interpretation functions (copy, reverse, shift, echo, swap, repeat, append, prepend, remove_first, remove_second; Figure 3) are exactly the kind of 'K independent latent string-transform rules applied to short string arguments' that c23 generalises into an induction-from-demos setting. c23's candidates.md explicitly names this paper as 'the direct template for c23's per-operator independent latent rules' (c23.md, Academic anchor). Both tasks are generator-native/synthetic with an independent deterministic oracle, both score by exact match on short transformed sequences, and both decompose difficulty by number-of-operations/length/depth (this paper's Figure 6 breakdown by length/depth/#functions is structurally the same difficulty ladder c23 proposes for its incremental prompt-quality gradient).
relation_to_candidate.differences: (1) Protocol: PCFG SET trains models from scratch on tens of thousands of labeled examples per condition; c23 is an in-context, no-gradient-update, few-shot-prompt-plus-query paradigm for already-trained (typically frozen, black-box) LLMs. (2) What varies across 'difficulty': PCFG SET varies training-set composition (holding out function pairs, exception frequency, synonym distribution) to probe a trained model's generalisation; c23 varies what the *prompt* states or lets the model induce about the rule set, since no training/fine-tuning occurs. (3) Rule discovery: PCFG SET's interpretation functions are always given to the (implicitly, via training data) model -- there is no rule-induction-from-few-shot-demos component, since the functions are named tokens in the input string itself (e.g. the token 'reverse' literally appears in the input); c23's central mechanic (inferring latent, unnamed rules purely from I/O demonstration pairs) has no counterpart here. (4) Multiple-choice: c23's 4-way MC-among-near-miss-distractors variant has no analogue in this paper, which only ever scores free-generation exact match or pairwise output consistency. (5) Verifier: this paper adds a 'consistency' metric (output-vs-own-earlier-output) that c23 does not use.
relation_to_candidate.what_c23_adds: c23 would add: (a) genuine latent-rule induction from unlabeled few-shot I/O pairs, with no rule names ever appearing in the prompt (PCFG SET's function names are literally present as input tokens, so no induction is required -- the model only needs to learn the meaning of a known symbol); (b) an in-context, gradient-free evaluation protocol suited to frozen LLMs rather than a from-scratch-training paradigm; (c) a smooth incremental prompt-quality ladder (state 0..K rules) as the main independent variable, versus PCFG SET's fixed interpretation functions and varying *training-distribution* difficulty; (d) a 4-way multiple-choice scoring option with distractors wrong in exactly one rule, which has no PCFG SET analogue; (e) c23's explicit contamination-proofing via randomized rule subsets over invented vocabularies per instance, whereas PCFG SET's string alphabet (520 symbols) and function set are fixed and public (released on GitHub), and the *evaluated* systems in this paper are trained on that exact released data, so PCFG SET as a static released corpus would not itself be contamination-safe as an LLM-prompting eval today without re-randomization -- a gap c23 is designed to close.

corpus_claim: PCFG SET / Compositionality Decomposed (Hupkes et al., JAIR 2020) provides string-edit transduction with five named generalization axes and is the direct template for c23's per-operator independent latent rules.
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported. The paper does verifiably provide (a) a string-edit transduction task (PCFG SET, Sec. 4) and (b) exactly five named generalization/compositionality axes -- systematicity, productivity, substitutivity, localism, overgeneralisation (Sec. 3, enumerated explicitly in the Abstract and Sec. 7.2) -- so that portion of the claim is fully verified. However, the claim that this is 'the direct template for c23's per-operator independent latent rules' overstates the mechanistic similarity: in PCFG SET the per-operator rules (copy, reverse, append, etc.) are NOT latent -- the operator name is an explicit token in the input sequence that the model must learn to interpret via supervised training on labeled data, not a hidden rule the model must induce purely from a handful of in-context I/O demonstrations with no gradient updates. c23's core mechanic (K independent latent rules that must be inferred from few-shot demos alone, at inference time, by a frozen model) has no direct counterpart in this paper's design; it is present only in a looser, thematic sense (independent named string-edit operations whose individual difficulty and compositional combination is separately probed). The claim is best read as 'templates the operator taxonomy and independent-axis decomposition style' rather than 'templates the induction-from-demos mechanic', so it is partially rather than fully supported as literally written.
baseline_relevant: String/argument-length dial: training data capped string arguments at length 5 (Sec. 4.3.2); all three architectures generalise poorly beyond that length, with LSTMS2S dropping to exactly 0% accuracy beyond length 5 and Transformer/ConvS2S degrading more gradually to 0 by length ~9-13 (Sec. 6.5.2) -- useful floor/ceiling reference for any baseline that scales c23's string-argument length as a difficulty dial.

Sequence-length/depth/#functions dial: accuracy for all architectures is near 1.0 for low depth/length/#functions and decays smoothly as these increase (Sec. 6.1.1, Figure 6) -- confirms length/depth/#operations is a reliable, smooth difficulty axis, mirroring c23's incremental-ladder design goal.

Metric definitions: 'sequence accuracy' = exact full-string match against target (marked * in Table 1); 'consistency score' = exact match between two model outputs on paired inputs, used when no single gold target is meaningful (marked † in Table 1) -- both are 0/1, no-partial-credit metrics, directly analogous to c23's proposed exact-match scoring.

Alphabet/vocabulary size used: 520 distinct string-argument symbols, ~100k total generated input-output pairs (85% train / 5% val / 10% test), explicit care taken that 'the same string arguments are never repeated' to make memorisation unattractive (Sec. 4.3.2) -- a directly relevant precedent for how large an invented vocabulary needs to be to defeat memorisation shortcuts.

Data/code release status: 'The data and scripts to run these experiments as well as the trained models are all available online' at https://github.com/i-machine-think/am-i-compositional (Sec. 6, footnote; also Sec. 7.3) -- this is a public, non-contamination-proof static release (fixed alphabet/grammar), so if used as-is for LLM evaluation today it would need re-randomization; c23's design of randomizing rule subsets over invented vocabularies per instance is the appropriate mitigation.

Exception-rate dial for overgeneralisation-style tests: authors grid-searched exception frequency at 0.01%, 0.05%, 0.1%, 0.5% of the least-frequent function's occurrences and found overgeneralisation collapses above ~0.5% and memorisation fails below ~0.01% (Sec. 6.6.1-6.6.2) -- relevant if c23 wants an analogous 'how much contradicting evidence before the rule is overridden' dial.

Ceiling caveat: even the best architecture (Transformer) plateaus well below 100% on every one of the five compositionality axes (task 0.92, systematicity 0.72, productivity 0.50, localism 0.54) despite unlimited supervised training data -- a cautionary data point for c23's own risk that 'the ceiling may be unreachable unless rule complexity is deliberately curated' (c23.md, Risks section), since even a fully-trained (not just prompted) model does not reach ceiling on the harder axes here.

No hyperparameter grid search was performed for any of the three architectures; authors explicitly warn results are illustrative of the test suite, not general architecture claims (Sec. 6 intro paragraph) -- relevant caveat for baseline-design rigor expectations.
quotes: "We collect different interpretations of compositionality and translate them into five theoretically grounded tests for models that are formulated on a task-independent level." (Abstract)

"We propose to test (i) if models systematically recombine known parts and rules (systematicity) (ii) if models can extend their predictions beyond the length they have seen in the training data (productivity) (iii) if models' predictions are robust to synonym substitutions (substitutivity) (iv) if models' composition operations are local or global (localism) and (v) if models favour rules or exceptions during training (overgeneralisation)." (Sec. 3)
"The meaning of a PCFG SET input sequence is constructed by recursively applying the string edit operations specified in the sequence." (Sec. 4.2)

"We set the size of the string alphabet to 520 and create a base corpus of around 100 thousand distinct input-output pairs. We limit the length of the string arguments given to the functions to 5." (Sec. 4.3.2)
"The Transformer outperforms both LSTMS2S and ConvS2S (p≈10^-4 and p≈10^-3, respectively), with a surprisingly high accuracy of 0.92." (Sec. 6.1)

"Intriguingly, the systematicity scores of all models are substantially lower than their overall task accuracies. This large difference is surprising, since PCFG SET is constructed such that a high task accuracy requires systematic recombination." (Sec. 6.2.2)

"For all runs, the performance of LSTMS2S immediately drops to zero when string arguments exceed length five, the maximum string length seen during training." (Sec. 6.5.2 / self-check verbatim spot-check)

"As described before, we did not run a grid-search to optimise the hyper-parameters of the three architectures we investigate ... these results serve as an illustration of the usefulness of our tests ... these results should not be taken as general claims about LSTMs, convolutional networks or transformers." (Sec. 6, introductory paragraph)

corrections: representations field overstated cross-architecture consistency of the per-function difficulty ranking by including `reverse` in the 'harder' tier for all architectures. The source (Sec. 6.1.2) explicitly calls out `reverse` as 'an interesting difference between architectures': it is difficult for LSTMS2S and ConvS2S but, for Transformer, is on par with the substantially easier functions echo/swap/shift, because Transformer's non-sequential attention is not bound to positional ordering the way the recurrent/convolutional models are. Removed `reverse` from the 'consistent harder' list and added an explicit exception clause with citation.
